summary: Fowler presents Jez Humble's simple three-question test for genuine Continuous Integration, revealing that most teams who claim to practice CI actually don't. The test asks: does everyone commit daily to a shared mainline, does each commit trigger automated builds/tests, and when builds fail, are they fixed within ten minutes? At conferences, most hands go up initially but only a few remain after all three questions. Fowler argues that running CI tools on feature branches is 'CI theater' that misses the point—true CI is about keeping everyone's code synchronized, not about tooling.
key_points: True CI requires daily commits to a shared mainline, automated builds on every commit, and fixing broken builds within ten minutes or reverting
Most teams who believe they practice CI actually fail this basic three-question test
Running CI servers on feature branches is 'CI theater' that provides none of the actual benefits
CI was originally defined by Kent Beck as a human workflow practice, not a tool category
We should resist semantic diffusion by continually re-explaining the proper meaning of CI rather than abandoning the term
money_quotes: Continuous Integration—as it was originally described and named by Kent Beck as part of Extreme Programming—has nothing to do with tools.
The whole idea is that nobody is working on a code base that deviates significantly from anyone else's.
Running such a process otherwise, such as on every Feature Branch, is CI theater that debases the name.
If you can't fix and get a green commit build within ten minutes, then you should revert to the last green build.
We shouldn't give in to semantic diffusion, instead we need to keep working at re-explaining the proper meaning of Continuous Integration.
Continuous Integration means the team knows what the current state of the code truly is, we avoid big risky merges, and people can refactor as much as they need to.
key_insight: True Continuous Integration is a discipline about keeping all developers synchronized on a shared mainline, not a category of tooling—and most teams who think they practice it actually don't.
